Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Islamic Tradition

al-Ghazālī

1058 – 1111

Skepticism, illumination, and the soul between theology and mystical knowledge.

Al-Ghazālī does not give us a “philosophy of mind” in the modern disciplinary sense. What he gives us is something broader and, in some respects, more demanding: a theory of the human subject as a knowing, willing, affective, morally transformable, spiritually illuminable creature situated between animality, angelic intelligence, and divine knowledge. The closest modern label would be a theocentric and ethical philosophy of mind, but that label should not flatten its religious structure. In Ghazālī’s own conceptual universe, the mind is not adequately understood until one has treated the heart, the soul, the intellect, imagination, desire, anger, prophecy, angelic mediation, divine illumination, and the diseases and purification of cognition.

The central reconstruction is this: for Ghazālī, the real human subject is a subtle created reality, most often called the qalb when considered as the locus of knowledge and moral address. The same human reality can also be called rūḥ, nafs, or ʿaql, depending on whether one is emphasizing life, selfhood, appetite, rational apprehension, or knowledge. The body is not the mind, and the brain is not the mind, but bodily organs, brain-based faculties, sensory powers, appetites, and habits are indispensable instruments of the mind’s worldly operation. Knowledge is not merely information-processing; it is the presence, disclosure, or “impression” of realities in the heart. Error is not merely bad inference; it may be opacity, rust, captivity to passion, imaginative distortion, or lack of divine light. Reason is a light, but not the final light. Prophecy is not irrationality but a higher cognitive mode. Angels are not decorative mythology but created noetic agents within the architecture of knowledge. God is not “a mind” alongside other minds, but the transcendent source of all light, being, knowledge, and intelligibility.

1. Intellectual setting: not anti-philosophy, but disciplined appropriation

A rigorous reading has to begin by resisting two caricatures. The first caricature is that Ghazālī is simply an anti-philosopher who destroyed philosophy. The second is that his theological and Sufi materials can be stripped away to recover a “serious” psychology underneath. Both readings miss the structure of the project.

Historically, Ghazālī knew the philosophical psychology of the Arabic Aristotelian and Avicennian tradition. The broader Islamic philosophical tradition treated the soul, internal senses, imagination, rational intellect, and the relation between human cognition and higher intelligences as major philosophical questions. Avicenna’s psychology, especially the distinction between external and internal senses and the immateriality of intellect, is part of the background against which Ghazālī writes. Ghazālī’s own relation to falsafa is complex: he rejects what he regards as unproven or religiously dangerous doctrines, but he also accepts and deploys philosophical logic, psychology, and metaphysics where he judges them useful or true.

This matters because Ghazālī’s philosophy of mind is neither a simple return to pre-philosophical piety nor a disguised Avicennian system. It is a critical reconfiguration. He takes over much of the philosophical vocabulary of faculties, intellect, imagination, and intelligible forms, but he relocates these within a Qurʾānic, theological, ethical, and Sufi account of purification, divine light, prophecy, and the afterlife. The result is not “psychology plus theology.” It is a unified anthropology in which cognition, salvation, character, and metaphysics are inseparable.

2. The four names of the subject: qalb, rūḥ, nafs, ʿaql

The most important place to begin is Ghazālī’s discussion of four terms in the Iḥyāʾ ʿulūm al-dīn, especially in the “Book of the Marvels of the Heart” (Kitāb sharḥ ʿajāʾib al-qalb). He explicitly warns that qalb, rūḥ, nafs, and ʿaql are multivocal terms. Confusion about these terms produces confusion about the human being.

The qalb has two principal meanings. One is the physical heart, the bodily organ. The other is a latīfa rabbāniyya rūḥāniyya, a subtle, lordly, spiritual reality. This second sense is the crucial one for philosophy of mind. It is the true human being: the perceiving, knowing, morally addressed subject. It has a relation to the physical heart, but Ghazālī refuses to reduce that relation to a simple physical explanation.

The rūḥ also has two meanings. It can refer to the subtle bodily spirit that spreads from the heart through the body and supports sensation and life. But it can also refer to the same knowing and perceiving subtle entity that Ghazālī has just called the heart. The nafs can mean the lower self, especially the complex of appetite and anger that must be disciplined, but it can also mean the human person’s true essence. The ʿaql can mean knowledge itself, or it can mean the knowing subject, again converging with the subtle human reality designated by qalb, rūḥ, and nafs in their higher senses.

This gives us the first systematic thesis:

The human mind is not one faculty among others. It is the real subject of perception, knowledge, responsibility, and transformation. The terms qalb, rūḥ, nafs, and ʿaql name this subject under different descriptions. “Heart” names it as the locus of disclosure and moral-spiritual orientation. “Spirit” names it as living subtle reality. “Self” names it either as the whole person or, in another register, as the lower appetitive self. “Intellect” names it as knower or as the knowledge by which it knows.

This is why translating qalb simply as “heart” can mislead if “heart” is heard as mere emotion. Translating ʿaql simply as “reason” can also mislead if “reason” is heard as discursive inference only. Ghazālī’s psychology is not a division between emotional heart and rational mind. The heart, in the relevant sense, is the knowing center of the person.

3. The body: instrument, city, mount, and battlefield

Ghazālī’s model is not materialist, but neither is it a casual dismissal of the body. The body is instrumentally indispensable. He repeatedly describes the bodily organs as the servants, soldiers, or instruments of the heart. The body is also the “mount” by which the human being travels; knowledge is the provision for the journey. This makes the body subordinate but not irrelevant. Without senses, bodily organs, habits, and disciplined action, the heart’s worldly cognition and moral development are impaired.

His analogies are precise. The body is like a city; the heart is like its king. Appetite and anger are not evil simply by existing; they are powers that must be governed. Appetite is like a horse: necessary for movement, but dangerous if uncontrolled. Anger is like a dog: useful when trained, destructive when it becomes master. Reason functions as governor, adviser, or minister. The moral problem is not that the embodied powers exist, but that they usurp sovereignty.

This yields a second thesis:

Ghazālī’s mind-body account is non-reductive embodiment. The body does not generate the true subject, but bodily powers provide the conditions through which the subject senses, imagines, desires, acts, sins, learns, purifies itself, and moves toward or away from God.

One should therefore avoid calling Ghazālī a simple dualist in the modern Cartesian sense. Cartesian dualism tends to imagine a thinking substance set over against extended matter. Ghazālī’s human subject is indeed irreducible to matter, but it is not a self-sufficient thinking ego. It is a morally vulnerable, divinely dependent, bodily situated, cosmically embedded heart. Its knowledge depends on sense, discipline, revelation, divine light, and sometimes angelic mediation. Its diseases are ethical and spiritual, not merely cognitive.

4. Faculty psychology: senses, imagination, memory, thought, will

Ghazālī incorporates a sophisticated faculty psychology. He distinguishes external senses from internal powers, and he assigns important roles to imagination, memory, recollection, and thought. In the Iḥyāʾ, he names internal faculties such as common sense, imagination, thought, recollection, and memory, and he associates them with the brain’s regions in a way that reflects the inherited medical-philosophical psychology of his period. Elsewhere in the same discussion, he compares the senses to spies, imagination to an official who preserves reports, memory to a treasurer, and the internal faculties to bureaucratic servants of the ruling heart.

This is important for a philosophy of mind because it shows that Ghazālī does not identify cognition with a single undifferentiated “soul.” He has a layered architecture:

The external senses receive the sensible world. Internal sense gathers and organizes sensory data. Imagination preserves and recombines images. Memory stores meanings or retained contents. Thought works through relations, inferences, and practical deliberations. Intellect apprehends universals and necessary truths. Will allows the subject to act against immediate appetite on the basis of rational or spiritual judgment. The heart is the central subject in which knowledge, orientation, and moral transformation occur.

Ghazālī’s account of will is especially philosophically important. He says that the human heart is distinguished by knowledge and will. Knowledge allows the person to grasp universals, consequences, and realities not given immediately to sensation. Will allows the person to pursue what intellect recognizes as beneficial even when appetite resists. His example is the person who undergoes bloodletting despite natural aversion because reason judges it medically beneficial.

This gives us a third thesis:

Human mindedness is marked by the capacity to subordinate immediate impulse to represented goods. Appetite seeks proximate satisfaction; intellect apprehends a broader order of benefit and harm; will translates that apprehension into action. Moral failure is therefore not just ignorance. It is often the failure of governance: appetite, anger, imagination, or habit overpowers the heart’s rational and spiritual orientation.

5. Knowledge as presence in the heart

Ghazālī’s theory of knowledge is often expressed through the mirror analogy. The heart is like a mirror; realities are like objects reflected in it. Knowledge occurs when the form, likeness, or disclosure of a reality is present in the heart. Ignorance or error can occur because the mirror is defective, dirty, turned in the wrong direction, veiled, or ignorant of where to look. Obedience, discipline, and purification polish the mirror.

This is not a crude picture theory. It is better understood as a theory of intentional presence: to know is for the known reality, or a true form corresponding to it, to become present to the heart. The heart is not merely a container of propositions. It is a receptive and active locus of disclosure. Its condition affects what can appear in it.

The mirror model gives us several components of a Ghazālian epistemology of mind.

First, cognition has an object-directed structure. The heart is directed toward realities outside itself.

Second, the knower’s state matters. Knowledge is not independent of the moral and spiritual condition of the subject.

Third, cognitive failure has multiple causes. It can arise from lack of innate capacity, moral corruption, misorientation, veiling, or ignorance of the method by which the object should be approached.

Fourth, purification is epistemic, not merely moral. Worship, discipline, repentance, and dhikr are not external religious ornaments added to a theory of knowledge; they are practices that alter the knower’s receptivity to truth.

This is one of the sharpest differences between Ghazālī and much modern philosophy of mind. Modern accounts often separate epistemology, ethics, and spiritual practice. Ghazālī treats them as mutually entangled. A vicious soul does not merely have bad preferences; it has damaged access to reality.

6. Reason, certainty, and the crisis of skepticism

Ghazālī’s autobiographical al-Munqidh min al-ḍalāl is indispensable for extracting his philosophy of mind because it describes a crisis in the foundations of cognition. He asks what kind of knowledge is certain enough that error becomes impossible. Sense perception proves unreliable. Even rational self-evidence becomes destabilized when he imagines that there may be a higher faculty that judges reason as reason judges sense. His recovery, famously, does not come from syllogistic proof alone but from a light God casts into the heart.

This episode should not be read as anti-rationalism in the crude sense. Ghazālī does not abandon reason. Rather, he denies that reason can ground itself absolutely by its own autonomous resources. Foundational certainty is restored by divine illumination, after which reason can function properly. This is why one can call his position illuminationist foundationalism, provided one does not confuse it with later technical Ishrāqī philosophy. The mind’s basic trust in necessary truths is itself dependent on divine generosity.

His account of intellectual development also matters. In al-Munqidh, he describes stages: sense perception, discrimination, intellect, and then a further faculty beyond intellect through which prophets perceive realities inaccessible to ordinary reason. That higher faculty does not abolish intellect; it exceeds it. Just as intellect reveals things unavailable to the senses, prophecy reveals things unavailable to ordinary intellect.

The resulting thesis is:

Reason is real light, but not final light. It is authoritative within its domain, indispensable for distinguishing truth from error, and necessary for interpreting revelation and experience. But it is not the highest possible cognitive mode. Prophetic cognition is suprarational, not irrational.

7. The Mishkāt al-anwār: mind as graded light

The Mishkāt al-anwār offers perhaps Ghazālī’s most philosophically dense model of mind. It begins from the Qurʾānic Light Verse and develops a hierarchy of light, perception, intellect, prophecy, angelic lights, and divine light. Ghazālī argues that physical light is called light because it makes visible things visible, but the eye is also a kind of light because it perceives. Still higher than the eye is intelligence, because it perceives what sense cannot: hidden realities, causes, universals, and intelligible relations.

He also makes a striking claim about reflexivity: intellect perceives itself and even knows its own knowing. This gives Ghazālī a theory of higher-order awareness. The intellect is not merely outward-facing. It has self-relation. It can know that it knows.

In the same work, he distinguishes five “spirits” or cognitive levels: the sensory spirit, the imaginative spirit, the intelligential spirit, the ratiocinative spirit, and the transcendental prophetic spirit. The sensory spirit receives sense data. The imaginative spirit preserves sensory forms when the objects are absent. The intelligential spirit apprehends meanings and divine ideas beyond sense and imagination. The ratiocinative spirit develops knowledge through inference. The prophetic spirit is possessed by prophets and some saints; it receives illumination without ordinary instruction.

This layered model gives us a fourth thesis:

Mindedness is graded luminosity. A being is “mind-like” to the extent that it perceives, discloses, understands, and participates in light. Physical sight is a low form of luminosity. Imagination is higher than raw sensation but still ambiguous. Intellect is higher because it apprehends universals and invisible realities. Prophetic spirit is higher still because it receives direct illumination.

The language of light is not just metaphorical decoration. It encodes Ghazālī’s epistemic ontology: to know is to be lit, and to be lit is to receive from a source higher than oneself. All finite cognition is borrowed light.

8. Imagination: necessary, dangerous, and spiritually transformable

Imagination has a double status. It is necessary because the human mind cannot operate in the sensible world without preserving, recombining, and symbolizing sensory forms. It is dangerous because it can dominate intellect, project bodily forms onto non-bodily realities, and trap the person in anthropomorphic or fantastical representations.

In the Mishkāt, Ghazālī says the imaginative spirit is initially opaque, like gross glass, because it is made from the stuff of the lower sensible world; yet it can be clarified and refined until it becomes transparent to intellectual light. This is a subtle theory. Imagination is not merely error. It is a medium that can either distort or transmit.

This matters especially for dreams, symbols, prophecy, and religious language. In the Iḥyāʾ, Ghazālī distinguishes two doors of the heart: one opening outward toward the sensory world and one opening inward toward the world of dominion or malakūt. Dreams, inspiration, and prophetic revelation show that not all knowledge comes by ordinary sensory learning.

A modern reconstruction might say that Ghazālī has a theory of symbolic cognition. The imagination can translate suprasensible meanings into sensible images. But because it can also literalize symbols and project bodily categories onto God or angels, it must be governed by intellect, revelation, and purification.

9. Desire, anger, attention, and moral psychology

Ghazālī’s philosophy of mind is also a philosophy of affect and motivation. Desire and anger are not incidental. They are central powers in the architecture of the person. When disciplined, they serve survival, action, courage, and the pursuit of goods. When undisciplined, they become tyrants.

His image of the heart as a fortress is revealing. The devil attacks through entrances such as anger and desire. Ghazālī’s claim that anger is the “ghoul” or destructive force of intellect is psychologically acute: anger narrows attention, distorts salience, and makes the intellect serve revenge rather than truth.

He also offers a nuanced account of the stages leading to action. Passing thoughts are not yet culpable. An involuntary inclination is not yet full responsibility. Culpability increases when the person entertains, consents to, resolves upon, and acts on a thought. This anticipates later distinctions between intrusive thought, desire, endorsement, intention, and action.

The relevant systematic thesis is:

For Ghazālī, the mind is not primarily a belief-machine; it is a field of attention, desire, suggestion, judgment, intention, and action. Thoughts arrive from different sources; desires stir; imagination colors them; intellect evaluates; will consents or refuses; action consolidates character.

This is why spiritual discipline is psychologically intelligible. Repeated action forms dispositions. Dispositions alter attention and salience. Altered attention changes what the heart receives and how it interprets what it receives. The ethical life is therefore a long-term reconfiguration of cognitive receptivity.

10. God and the “mind of God”: necessary care

The user’s instruction is exactly right: comments about God and angels should not be dismissed as theological distractions. In Ghazālī, they are structurally central. But they must be handled with conceptual care.

If one uses the phrase “mind of God” in a philosophy-of-mind reconstruction, it must be used analogically and with restraint. God is not, for Ghazālī, a finite subject with mental states comparable to ours. God is the Real Light, the source of all light, the ground of being, knowledge, and intelligibility. Created lights are borrowed; divine light is not borrowed. In the Mishkāt, all other lights derive from the ultimate source, and only God is light in the fullest sense.

This does not make divine knowledge irrelevant to philosophy of mind. Quite the opposite. Divine knowledge is the metaphysical horizon within which all creaturely cognition is possible. The human heart knows because it is a created recipient of light. Prophetic knowledge occurs because God discloses. Certainty is restored because God casts light into the heart. Moral purification matters because it makes the heart receptive to divine guidance. The Qurʾān itself is compared to the sun by which the eye of intellect sees.

At the same time, Ghazālī warns against anthropomorphic projection. In the Mishkāt, he criticizes those who imagine divine hearing, sight, or life according to human bodily models, or who are veiled by false syllogisms and imagination. Divine attributes must not be assimilated to human faculties.

The resulting thesis is:

God is not one mind within the hierarchy of minds; God is the transcendent source of the hierarchy itself. Divine knowledge, will, life, and speech are not psychological states in the human sense. Yet any Ghazālian philosophy of mind that omits divine light loses the principle by which mind becomes intelligible.

11. Angelic minds: noetic agents, not ornaments

Angels are equally important. In the Iḥyāʾ, Ghazālī identifies a good thought’s source as angelic and describes the angel as a created being whose task includes pouring forth good, giving knowledge, and unveiling truth. In al-Munqidh, he describes the Sufi path as involving experiences in which one comes to witness angels and the spirits of prophets, while insisting that direct taste or experience differs from mere proof or testimony.

In the Mishkāt, angels belong to the higher realm and participate in the hierarchy of lights. Ghazālī speaks of supernal or angelic spirits, ranks of celestial lights, and the descent of guidance or illumination through higher orders. This language overlaps with philosophical cosmology while being reinterpreted through Qurʾānic and Sufi symbolism.

For philosophy of mind, angels function in at least four ways.

First, they are models of cognition less entangled with appetite. Human beings stand between beasts and angels. A human who lets appetite and anger dominate falls toward animality or worse; a human who orders the faculties toward knowledge and obedience approaches angelic likeness.

Second, angels are noetic intermediaries. They are not merely objects of belief but agents in the economy of knowledge, inspiration, and moral suggestion.

Third, they help explain why cognition is not confined to the individual human organism. Ghazālī’s cognitive world is relational and vertical. Thoughts may arise from bodily appetite, demonic suggestion, angelic inspiration, sensory experience, rational inference, prophetic teaching, or divine illumination.

Fourth, angels provide a non-human case of created mindedness. They matter for any comprehensive Ghazālian philosophy of mind because they show that mind is not coextensive with human embodiment. There can be created intellective life whose relation to appetite, sensation, and body is different from ours.

The thesis here is:

Ghazālī’s philosophy of mind is a multi-species noetics: human minds, angelic intelligences, prophetic cognition, and divine knowledge belong to one ordered but asymmetrical field. Modern philosophy of mind often begins from the human organism. Ghazālī begins from an ontological hierarchy of light and asks where the human heart stands within it.

12. Prophecy as cognitive expansion

Prophecy is one of the strongest tests of whether we are reading Ghazālī on his own terms. If prophecy is treated merely as a dogmatic addition, his psychology becomes incoherent. For him, prophecy is a cognitive mode. It is not reducible to inference, imagination, or ordinary learning, though it may use imagination symbolically and reason interpretively.

In al-Munqidh, he argues by analogy: the person limited to sense might deny the deliverances of intellect; likewise, the person limited to ordinary intellect might deny prophecy. But the existence of a higher faculty should not be rejected merely because one has not personally experienced it. This is why he distinguishes proof, testimony, and taste. One can know about drunkenness by definition, believe reports about it, or experience it directly. Similarly, one can reason about prophecy, accept testimony concerning it, or taste something of the Sufi path.

The Mishkāt’s “transcendental prophetic spirit” makes the same point in another idiom. Some minds receive illumination without ordinary instruction; these are “almost lit” even before an external flame touches them. This is not a rejection of learning, but a claim that human cognition has an upper limit that prophecy surpasses.

A Ghazālian philosophy of mind therefore includes:

ordinary cognition, through sense, imagination, memory, and inference;

rational cognition, through universals, necessity, and demonstration;

purified cognition, through the polished heart;

inspired cognition, through angelic or divine suggestion;

prophetic cognition, through a higher faculty that discloses unseen realities.

This hierarchy is central, not peripheral.

13. The soul after death and the perfection of cognition

Ghazālī’s philosophy of mind is also eschatological. The human subject is not exhausted by bodily life. The body is the mount; the soul is the traveler. The worldly life is the period in which faculties, habits, knowledge, and character are formed. The afterlife is not merely a future location; it is the unveiling of the truth of what the heart has become.

In the Mishkāt, Ghazālī remarks that some errors of intellect arise because imagination and fantasy deceive it, and that separation from these lower deceptions is perfected after death. This suggests that death is not merely biological cessation but a transformation in the conditions of cognition.

This does not make the body insignificant. On the contrary, embodied life is where the heart is trained or ruined. But the final measure of the person is not bodily performance alone; it is the state of the subtle subject that has either polished or darkened itself.

14. What kind of “mind” theory is this?

If one extracts Ghazālī’s position into modern categories, the result is not one familiar theory but a composite with distinctive theses.

It is anti-reductionist: mind is not identical with brain, bodily motion, or sensory processing.

It is embodied: the mind operates through bodily organs, senses, appetites, and brain-based internal faculties.

It is faculty-theoretic: sensation, imagination, memory, thought, intellect, will, appetite, and anger have distinguishable roles.

It is intentional: knowledge is directed toward realities and consists in their presence or representation in the heart.

It is virtue-epistemological: the knower’s moral-spiritual condition affects access to truth.

It is illuminationist: certainty and higher knowledge ultimately depend on divine light.

It is hierarchical: cognition ranges from sensation to imagination, intellect, prophetic spirit, angelic light, and divine light.

It is therapeutic: diseases of the heart require diagnosis and treatment, not merely argument.

It is theological without being intellectually evasive: God and angels are not gaps in the theory; they are constitutive of its account of knowledge, suggestion, inspiration, and ultimate intelligibility.

A useful label would be purificatory noetics: an account of mind in which the central problem is how the human subject becomes capable of receiving truth.

15. Ghazālī and Avicenna: dependence, critique, transformation

The relation to Avicenna is one of the most important scholarly problems. Ghazālī clearly inherits parts of Avicennian psychology: the internal senses, the distinction between sense and intellect, the hierarchy of cognitive powers, and the idea that human knowledge can be related to higher intelligences. Yet he also transforms the system by putting the heart, prophecy, divine light, moral discipline, and religious practice at the center.

Modern scholarship reflects this tension. Some interpreters emphasize that Ghazālī’s “heart” often functions as an Islamicized term for Avicennian noetics. Others argue that his “pectoral psychology” is not merely a change of vocabulary but a genuine psychology in which the heart is more than an intellective faculty. Recent work on Ghazālī’s heart-centered psychology stresses that the heart is the essence of the human subject, the locus of knowledge, moral formation, and illumination, not merely a synonym for discursive reason.

This debate matters for extraction. A purely Avicennian reading risks reducing Ghazālī’s religious language to borrowed philosophical structures. A purely devotional reading risks missing the technical sophistication of his faculty psychology. The best reconstruction is integrative: Ghazālī absorbs philosophical psychology, criticizes its pretensions, and reorders it around the heart’s purification and dependence on divine light.

16. Causality and mental causation

The question of causality complicates any Ghazālian philosophy of mind. Ghazālī famously attacks the philosophers’ claim that causal connections are necessary in themselves. In modern terms, this affects mind-body causation, habit formation, perception, and action. If bodily events and mental events are not connected by autonomous necessity, then their conjunction depends on God’s creative ordering.

However, it would be too simplistic to say that Ghazālī denies all secondary causation in every sense. Modern scholarship continues to debate whether he is a strict occasionalist or whether he allows real secondary causes that do not necessitate their effects independently of God. A careful formulation is that, for Ghazālī, no created cause has self-sufficient necessitating power; all causal order remains dependent on divine will.

For philosophy of mind, this means that perception, action, bodily discipline, and spiritual exercises can be treated as reliable ordered means without becoming metaphysically autonomous mechanisms. The heart is polished through practices, but those practices do not compel divine illumination as a mechanical effect. They prepare receptivity; God gives light.

17. Non-English reception and interpretive emphases

The non-English material is genuinely useful because it often foregrounds aspects that Anglophone treatments subordinate to the Avicenna debate.

Contemporary Arabic reception often emphasizes Ghazālī’s differentiation of nafs, rūḥ, qalb, and ʿaql as a way of avoiding a flat psychology in which all mental life is reduced to “reason.” Some Arabic discussions also frame his project as a “believing rationality”: reason and revelation are not enemies, but reason has limits and prophecy opens a higher domain. This reception usefully preserves the unity of rational, ethical, and religious concerns, though it can sometimes become apologetic if it understates the philosophical tensions.

Arabic commentary on the Mishkāt also tends to stress the distinction between sensory sight and rational or spiritual light: the eye sees only the external surface, while intellect penetrates meanings, relations, and realities; God alone is true light in the highest sense. This aligns closely with the primary text and helps resist overly secularized readings of Ghazālī’s light metaphysics.

Turkish scholarship often treats Ghazālī’s psychology as part of an anthropology of knowing and acting: the concepts of rūḥ, nafs, and ʿaql are not isolated metaphysical labels but elements in an account of the human being as both knower and moral agent. This is a productive emphasis because it keeps cognition and action together.

French-language treatments, especially those focused on the relation between reason, transmitted revelation, and purification of the heart, tend to emphasize that rational sciences are necessary but insufficient. The intellect and religious transmission need one another; pure imitation is defective, but purely autonomous reason is also inadequate. French discussions of al-Ghazālī’s terminology also help highlight a translation problem: ʿaql can be rendered as raison, intelligence, intellect, or even knowledge depending on context.

Some Indonesian reception reads Ghazālī through modern neuroscience and psychology, interpreting nafs in relation to emotion and qalb as a spiritual-moral regulator of information and behavior. This is useful as contemporary application, but it should be treated cautiously. It is not what Ghazālī himself means by neuroscience; it is a modern mapping of Ghazālian categories onto current psychological interests.

Taken together, these non-English discussions support a broad conclusion: outside narrowly Anglophone debates, Ghazālī is often read less as a “critic of philosophy” and more as a theorist of the human being whose psychology is simultaneously rational, ethical, spiritual, and pedagogical.

18. The extracted system in concise theses

A comprehensive Ghazālian philosophy of mind can be stated as the following set of theses. 1. The real subject is the subtle heart. The human person’s deepest reality is not the physical heart, brain, or body, but a subtle spiritual entity that knows, wills, is addressed, and is morally responsible. 2. The same subject is named under several descriptions. Qalb, rūḥ, nafs, and ʿaql are not four separate substances. In their higher senses, they converge on the same human reality; in their lower or technical senses, they name bodily spirit, appetite, selfhood, knowledge, or rational capacity. 3. Embodiment is instrumental and formative. The body is not the mind, but the mind’s worldly operation depends on bodily faculties, senses, habits, appetite, anger, and brain-based internal powers. 4. Cognition is hierarchical. Sensation, imagination, memory, inference, intellect, unveiling, and prophecy are ordered levels, not interchangeable activities. 5. Knowledge is presence in the heart. To know is for a reality, or a form corresponding to it, to be disclosed in the heart. 6. The knower’s condition affects knowledge. Sin, passion, anger, pride, and heedlessness darken cognition; repentance, worship, discipline, and remembrance polish it. 7. Reason is indispensable but limited. It apprehends universals, judges, infers, and guards against error, but it neither grounds itself absolutely nor exhausts possible cognition. 8. Prophecy is a cognitive faculty or mode. It discloses what ordinary intellect cannot reach, just as intellect discloses what sense cannot reach. 9. Imagination is ambivalent. It can distort suprasensible realities by bodily projection, but it can also become transparent to higher meanings and serve symbolic cognition. 10. Mental life is morally contested. Thoughts, suggestions, impulses, intentions, and actions arise in a field where angelic inspiration, demonic whispering, bodily appetite, rational judgment, and divine guidance can all be relevant. 11. Angels are part of the noetic order. They are created intelligences and sources of good suggestion, knowledge, and unveiling; they also represent forms of mindedness not structured like human embodied appetite. 12. God is the source of intelligibility. Divine knowledge is not human mentality magnified. God is the transcendent light by which all created knowing becomes possible. 13. The aim of mind is not mere representation but felicity. The perfected mind is the purified heart that knows God, acts rightly, and becomes fit for nearness.

19. Relation to contemporary philosophy of mind

Ghazālī can be brought into conversation with contemporary philosophy of mind, but only if one avoids forcing him into alien categories.

Against reductive physicalism, he insists that the real subject is not identical with bodily mechanisms. Against a crude disembodied dualism, he insists that cognition is mediated by senses, imagination, habit, and bodily discipline. Against computational theories that treat cognition as formal manipulation alone, he makes the moral condition of the knower epistemically relevant. Against secular internalism, he treats knowledge as dependent on divine light, revelation, and sometimes angelic mediation. Against purely propositional epistemology, he distinguishes proof, testimony, and direct taste or experience.

The closest modern analogues are partial: virtue epistemology, because character affects knowing; embodied cognition, because bodily faculties matter; phenomenology, because disclosure and presence are central; externalism, because the sources of cognition exceed the individual organism; and non-reductive spiritual anthropology, because the subject cannot be reduced to matter. None of these fully captures him.

His theory is most distinctive where modern philosophy is often least comfortable: the mind’s highest function is not autonomous self-expression or neutral representation, but becoming receptive to truth, purified from distortion, and illuminated by God.

20. Major cautions for rigorous use

Several cautions are necessary.

First, not every work attributed to Ghazālī is equally secure, and not every genre has the same purpose. A systematic reconstruction should rely most heavily on the Iḥyāʾ, Mishkāt, al-Munqidh, and carefully contextualized material from the philosophical and theological works.

Second, the Maqāṣid al-falāsifa reports philosophical doctrines and was sometimes mistaken in Latin and Hebrew reception for Ghazālī’s own philosophical position. It is useful evidence of what he understood, not always of what he endorsed.

Third, the Tahāfut al-falāsifa should not be read as a rejection of all philosophy. Ghazālī attacks specific metaphysical claims that he considers unproven or incompatible with Islam; he does not reject logic, disciplined reasoning, or psychological analysis.

Fourth, “heart” must not be sentimentalized. It is not mere feeling. It is the knowing and morally responsible center.

Fifth, “intellect” must not be narrowed to discursive reasoning. Sometimes ʿaql means knowledge, sometimes the knower, sometimes rational faculty, and sometimes the light by which truth is apprehended.

Sixth, “prophecy beyond reason” must not be mistranslated as irrationalism. Ghazālī’s point is that reason has limits, not that reason is worthless.

Seventh, divine and angelic cognition must not be psychologized reductively. God is not a large human mind; angels are not metaphors only. They belong to the metaphysical and noetic architecture of his account.

Final formulation

The philosophy of mind one can extract from Ghazālī is a hierarchical, heart-centered, illuminationist, virtue-epistemological theory of the soul. Its subject is the subtle human heart; its instruments are bodily and psychic faculties; its ordinary operations include sensation, imagination, memory, inference, desire, anger, intention, and will; its epistemic goal is true disclosure of reality; its ethical condition is purification; its higher possibility is prophetic or inspired cognition; its supra-human ecology includes angels; and its ultimate ground is God, the Real Light.

In modern terms, Ghazālī’s deepest claim is that the mind is not understood by asking only what it represents, how it computes, or where it is located. One must also ask what it loves, what governs it, what has darkened it, what can purify it, what lights it, and toward what reality it is finally ordered.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
